methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $23M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Housing & Construction (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 1 same-sector peers exist in Housing & Construction, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Cover
private.rationale: Same sector (Housing & Construction), same stage (Series B)

data.narrative:
Cuby Technologies is a Series B-stage housing and construction company founded by Aleks Gampel and Aleh Kandrashou, having raised $23.4M to date. The sector peer universe is limited: two private comparables (ICON and Cover) exist, with zero public housing/construction firms identified as sufficiently comparable. This scarcity constrains traditional peer-median valuation approaches.

Stage-progression methodology is the primary applicable valuation lever. Using total capital raised ($23.4M) as a proxy for Series B enterprise value and applying a 1.9x multiplier—representing typical step-up magnitude to the next funding round—yields an implied valuation of $44M. This method rests on the assumption that the company's pro-rata ownership dilution and value creation track sector norms for the B→C transition. Peer-median analysis was not applicable due to insufficient comparable company depth: while one same-sector peer was identified, the narrow filter of stage-aligned, tier-similar companies fell short of the three-point minimum required for statistical reliability. Broader peer-set averaging would introduce unacceptable sector and stage heterogeneity.

The triangulated analysis supports a valuation range of $38M (low) to $51M (high), with $44M as the median point. This band reflects ±15% sensitivity around the stage-progression result. Key uncertainties include the representativeness of the 1.9x multiplier for this specific subsector, unobserved operating metrics (revenue, growth rate, burn), and the sparse comparables universe. Validation would benefit from revenue multiple calibration once additional Housing & Construction peer data becomes available.
